6.0: Testing how production will work (with seabream)

FAO Climate Change Risks to Fed Mariculture

Author

Tormey Reimer

Published

10 June 2026

← Previous: Extracting future temperatures

Next: Home →

Overview

This document runs the production for the seabream model.

Species and feed parameters

Load species parameters for seabream 
species_params <- file.path(prepdata_path, "species_parameters", "species_parameters_seabream.qs") %>% 
  qd_read()
Load feed and ingredient data and calculate feed composition parameters 
feeds_df <- read_excel(here("data", "raw_data", "feed_profiles_and_composition.xlsx"), sheet = "all_feeds") %>%
  select(feed, ingredient, proportion) %>% 
  filter(feed == "Gilthead seabream 1")

ingredients_df <- read_excel(here("data", "raw_data", "all_ingredients.xlsx"), sheet = "all_ingredients") %>% 
  select(ingredient, protein, lipid, carb)

digestibility_df <- read_excel(here("data", "raw_data", "all_ingredients.xlsx"), sheet = "all_ingredients") %>% 
  select(ingredient, mean_protein_digestibility, mean_lipid_digestibility, mean_carb_digestibility) %>% 
  rename(
    protein_digestibility = mean_protein_digestibility, 
    lipid_digestibility = mean_lipid_digestibility, 
    carb_digestibility = mean_carb_digestibility
  )

feed_params <- sum_feeds(
  format_feeds(feeds_df, ingredients_df, digestibility_df)
  )[[1]]

Temperatures

Load temperatures for all farms assigned for seabram 
farm_temps <- qs2::qs_read(file.path(prepdata_path, "farm_temps_bymodel", "farm_temps_seabream.qs")) %>% 
  sf::st_drop_geometry()
Pick a subset so this test runs faster 
farm_IDs_subset <- farm_temps %>% 
  distinct(ISO3_Code, farm_ID) %>% 
  group_by(ISO3_Code) %>% 
  slice_sample(n = 1) %>% 
  ungroup() %>% pull(farm_ID)
 
farm_temps <- farm_temps %>% 
  filter(farm_ID %in% farm_IDs_subset)
farm_temps <- split(farm_temps, farm_temps$farm_ID)
Show how farm temperatures change over time 
temp <- farm_temps %>% 
  bind_rows() %>% 
  mutate(year = year(date)) %>% 
  group_by(country, year) %>% 
  reframe(sst = mean(sst))
temp_2025 <- temp %>% 
  filter(year == 2025) %>% 
  rename(sst_2025 = sst) %>% 
  select(-year)

temp <- temp %>% 
  left_join(temp_2025, by = "country") %>% 
  mutate(sst_change = sst - sst_2025)

temp %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = sst_change, colour = country)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.75) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, colour = "black", linewidth = 0.5, linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(-5, 5, 0.5)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2025, 2100, 10)) +
  prettyplot() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom") +
  labs(x = "Date", y = "Change in farm SST (annual mean)")

Species production

Per-farm production has been set in the data based on current FAO production statistics. We are going to upscale the individual bioenergetic model to each farm by setting a per-farm population (based in 2025), accounting for expected per-annum mortality.

Set the grow-times for the species - essentially, the times where it will be most optimal at each farm 
# ft <- farm_temps[[25]]
farm_temps <- farm_temps %>% 
  map(function(ft) {
    # Isolate the temperatures at this farm
    temp <- ft %>% 
      sf::st_drop_geometry() %>% 
      filter(year == 2025) %>% 
      mutate(doy = as.integer(format(date, "%j"))) %>% 
      select(doy, sst)

    # Repeat year
    temp <- rbind(
      temp, 
      temp %>% mutate(doy = doy + 365)
    )

    # Get feeding response at each DOY
    temp$fr <- sapply(
        X = temp$sst, 
        FUN = feeding_rate, 
        species_params = species_params
      )
    temp$fr[is.na(temp$fr)] <- 0

    # Get the best "growing window" for this species
    grow_days <- species_params[["gd"]] %>% as.integer()
    gds <- temp %>% 
      mutate(
        roll_fr = slide_dbl(fr, sum, .before = 0, .after = grow_days - 1)
      ) %>%
      # Remove windows that don't have a full 653 days ahead
      slice(1:(n() - grow_days + 1)) %>%
      slice_max(roll_fr, n = 1) %>%
      mutate(end_doy = doy + grow_days - 1)

    ft %>% 
      mutate(
        doy_start = gds$doy,
        doy_end = gds$end_doy
      )
  },
  .progress = T
)

# Summarise
farm_temps %>% 
  bind_rows() %>% 
  group_by(country) %>% 
  reframe(
    mean_start = mean(doy_start),
    min_start = min(doy_start),
    max_start = max(doy_start),
    mean_end = mean(doy_end),
    min_end = min(doy_end),
    max_end = max(doy_end)
  )
# A tibble: 32 × 7
   country            mean_start min_start max_start mean_end min_end max_end
   <chr>                   <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>
 1 Albania                    98        98        98      730     730     730
 2 Algeria                    91        91        91      723     723     723
 3 Bahrain                     1         1         1      633     633     633
 4 China                      72        72        72      704     704     704
 5 Croatia                    98        98        98      730     730     730
 6 Cyprus                     98        98        98      730     730     730
 7 Dominican Republic          1         1         1      633     633     633
 8 Egypt                      98        98        98      730     730     730
 9 France                     98        98        98      730     730     730
10 Greece                     98        98        98      730     730     730
# ℹ 22 more rows
Assign a population to each farm to acheive expected production (using 2025 temperatures) 
# Running this chunk takes about 20 seconds but it's too large to cache with Quarto

# ft <- farm_temps[[1]]
farm_temps_pops <- farm_temps %>% 
  map(function(ft) {
    times <- c(
      t_start = unique(ft$doy_start),
      t_end   = unique(ft$doy_end),
      dt      = 1L
    )
    doys <- seq(times['t_start'], times['t_end'], times['dt'])

    # Filter to actual days in use
    ft_gd <- ft %>% 
      sf::st_drop_geometry() %>% 
      filter(year == 2025) %>% 
      mutate(doy = as.integer(format(date, "%j")))
    ft_gd <- rbind(
      ft_gd,
      ft_gd %>% mutate(doy = doy + 365)
    ) %>% 
      filter(doy %in% doys)

    # Get harvest size at this farm
    one_fish <- fish_growth(
      species_params = species_params,
      water_temp     = ft_gd$sst,
      feed_params    = feed_params,
      times          = times,
      init_weight    = species_params["meanW"],    # Use mean stocking weight
      ingmax         = species_params["meanImax"]  # Use mean ingestion coefficient
    ) %>% as.data.frame()

    # IMPORTANT - the number of grow_days is changed here, so that the fish gets as close to CS as possible
    harvest_doy <- one_fish$days[which.min(abs(one_fish$weight - species_params["CS"]))]
    species_params["gd"] <- length(seq(times['t_start'], harvest_doy, times['dt']))       # update species params
    ft$doy_end <- ft$doy_start + species_params["gd"] - 1

    harvest_size <- one_fish$weight[one_fish$days == harvest_doy]
    harvest_t <- unique(ft$prod_perfarm) %>% set_units("t") %>% set_units("g") %>% drop_units()
    ft$harvest_n <- harvest_t/harvest_size
    ft
  },
  .progress = T
)

Maintaining consistency through the future

If we just run the model now, we are going to run into the problem that warmer temperatures -> smaller fish (especially for salmon, a cold-water species), resulting in lower production. This will confound feed required, as smaller individuals eat less BUT warmer individuals have higher metabolism and eat more. Therefore, we need to explicitly decide on what to change and what to keep constant through time.

Total harvested biomass = Individual size (after grow days) Population*

  • Option 1: Let total harvested biomass adapt. Keep all individual and population parameters fixed through time, and let total harvested biomass change with temperature. While this might be the simplest option to implement/understand, it’s possibly also the least realistic as it assumes that fish farmers will take no adaptive action to prevent their harvests from declining.
  • Option 2: Let fish grow out as needed. Keep a fixed population and total harvested biomass, and adjust the length of the grow-out period to acheive the required total harvested biomass. On an individual farm level this makes sense (a farmer will just let his stock keep growing until they reach market size), but creates problems from a modelling perspective - fish may never reach market size if temperature changes to much, or a 2-year cohort might become a 3-year cohort (causing problems with reaching the annual production target).
  • Option 3: Let population absorb the difference. Keep a fixed number of grow-out days, and change farm population to maintain the same total harvested biomass. This possibly makes the most sense, as “population” is an arbitrary calculation used to reach total harvested biomass.
Run the model with all species and population parameters fixed through time 
# ftp <- farm_temps_pops[[1]]
option_1_changes <- map_dfr(farm_temps_pops, function (ftp) {
  # start_year <- (2025:2098)[1]
  map_dfr(2025:2098, function(start_year) {
    ftpy <- ftp %>%
      filter(year %in% c(start_year, start_year + 1)) %>% 
      mutate(
        doy = as.integer(format(date, "%j")),
        doy = case_when(
          year == start_year ~ doy, 
          leap_year(date) ~ doy + 366,
          T ~ doy + 365
        )
      ) %>% 
      filter(doy >= doy_start)
    ftpy <- ftpy[1:species_params["gd"], ] # required because some years are leap years, so the end date will be a little different
    times <- c(t_start = unique(ftpy$doy_start), t_end = (unique(ftpy$doy_start) + nrow(ftpy) - 1), dt = 1L)
    
    results_full <- farm_growth_full(
      species_params = species_params, 
      feed_params = feed_params, 
      water_temp = ftpy$sst, 
      times = times,
      use_MC_population = F
    )
    results_consolidate <- consolidate_farm(results_full)
    results_farm <- scale_farm_population(
      consolidated = results_consolidate, 
      N_pop = generate_pop(
        harvest_n = unique(ftpy$harvest_n), 
        mort = species_params["mortmyt"], 
        times = times
      )
    )

    data.frame(
      start_year = start_year,
      individual_weight = last(results_consolidate[["stats"]][["weight"]][,1]),
      farm_biomass = last(results_farm[["stats"]][["weight_scaled"]][,1]),
      farm_food_prov = sum(results_farm[["stats"]][["food_prov_scaled"]][,1], na.rm = T)
    )
  }) %>% 
    mutate(
      country = unique(ftp$country),
      farm_ID = unique(ftp$farm_ID)
    )
},
.progress = T
)

qd_save(
  option_1_changes,
  file.path(data_path, "intermediates", "option_1_changes.qs")
)

As expected, individual biomass decreases over time due to increasing SST (Figure 2). This results in declining farmed biomass (with the same population) and decreasing total feed provided. However, feed provided per unit of biomass produced increases.

Look at option 1 outcomes 
option_1_changes <- file.path(data_path, "intermediates", "option_1_changes.qs") %>% 
  qd_read() %>%  
  mutate(country = as.factor(country))

p1 <- ggplot(option_1_changes, aes(x = start_year, y = individual_weight, colour = country)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.75) +
  labs(x = "Start year", y = "Individual weight", title = "Individual weight by year") +
  prettyplot()

p2 <- ggplot(option_1_changes, aes(x = start_year, y = farm_biomass, colour = country)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.75) +
  labs(x = "Start year", y = "Farm biomass", title = "Farm biomass by year") +
  prettyplot()

p3 <- ggplot(option_1_changes, aes(x = start_year, y = farm_food_prov, colour = country)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.75) +
  labs(x = "Start year", y = "Feed provided", title = "Feed provided by year") +
  prettyplot()

p4 <- ggplot(option_1_changes, aes(x = farm_biomass, y = farm_food_prov, colour = country)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.75) +
  labs(x = "Farm biomass", y = "Feed provided", title = "Feed provided by farm biomass") +
  prettyplot()

(p1 + p2) / (p3 + p4) +
  plot_layout(guides = "collect") &
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
Run the model with all species and population parameters fixed through time, except for the growout period which changes to adapt with the climate 
# ftp <- farm_temps_pops[[1]]
option_2_changes <- map_dfr(farm_temps_pops, function (ftp) {
  # start_year <- (2025:2097)[1]
  map_dfr(2025:2097, function(start_year) {
    ftpy <- ftp %>%
      filter(year %in% c(start_year, start_year + 1, start_year + 2)) %>% 
      mutate(
        doy = as.integer(format(date, "%j")),
        doy = case_when(
          year == start_year ~ doy,                                                                     # days in 2025 keep their current doy
          leap_year(date) & year == (start_year + 1) ~ doy + 366,                                       # days in 2026 get an extra doy IF 2025 is a leap year
          (leap_year(start_year) | leap_year(start_year + 1)) & year == (start_year + 2) ~ doy + 731,   # days in 2027 get an extra doy IF 2025 OR 2026 is a leap year
          year == (start_year + 1) ~ doy + 365,                                                         # if no leap years, days in 2026 get doy + 365
          year == (start_year + 2) ~ doy + 730,                                                         # if no leap years, days in 2027 get doy + 730
        )
      ) %>% 
      filter(doy >= doy_start)
    ftpy <- ftpy[1:(species_params["gd"] + 60), ] # required because some years are leap years, so the end date will be a little different - AND give leeway of two months to allow for a changing number of growout days
    times <- c(t_start = unique(ftpy$doy_start), t_end = (unique(ftpy$doy_start) + nrow(ftpy) - 1), dt = 1L)
    
    results_full <- farm_growth_full(
      species_params = species_params, 
      feed_params = feed_params, 
      water_temp = ftpy$sst, 
      times = times,
      use_MC_population = F
    )
    results_consolidate <- consolidate_farm(results_full)

    # Adapt growout days to reach CS
    reached_CS <- which.min(abs(results_consolidate[["stats"]][["weight"]][,1] - species_params["CS"]))
    growdays <- results_consolidate[["days"]][reached_CS]
    results_consolidate_2 <-  list(
      days  = results_consolidate$days[1:reached_CS],
      stats = purrr::map(results_consolidate$stats, \(m) m[1:reached_CS, , drop = FALSE])
    )

    results_farm <- scale_farm_population(
      consolidated = results_consolidate_2, 
      N_pop = generate_pop(
        harvest_n = unique(ftpy$harvest_n), 
        mort = species_params["mortmyt"], 
        times = times
      )
    )

    data.frame(
      start_year = start_year,
      days_to_harvest = reached_CS,
      individual_weight = results_consolidate_2[["stats"]][["weight"]][reached_CS, 1],
      farm_biomass = results_farm[["stats"]][["weight_scaled"]][reached_CS, 1],
      farm_food_prov = sum(results_farm[["stats"]][["food_prov_scaled"]][1:reached_CS, 1], na.rm = T)
    )
  }) %>% 
    mutate(
      country = unique(ftp$country),
      farm_ID = unique(ftp$farm_ID)
    )
},
.progress = T
)

qd_save(
  option_2_changes,
  file.path(data_path, "intermediates", "option_2_changes.qs")
)

Note that for the purposes of demonstrating option 2 the time series only go up to 2097 - this is because the growout days eventually exceeded 730 days (2 years). This is going to be an issue for the final production runs, as our target harvest biomass is predicated on an annual production from 2-year cohorts.

The results of Option 2 look very similar to Option 1 (Figure 3). Despite the changes in…

Look at option 2 outcomes 
option_2_changes <- file.path(data_path, "intermediates", "option_2_changes.qs") %>% 
  qd_read() %>%  
  mutate(country = as.factor(country))

p1 <- ggplot(option_2_changes, aes(x = start_year, y = days_to_harvest, colour = country)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.75) +
  labs(x = "Start year", y = "Growout days", title = "Growout days by year") +
  prettyplot()

p2 <- ggplot(option_2_changes, aes(x = start_year, y = farm_biomass, colour = country)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.75) +
  labs(x = "Start year", y = "Farm biomass", title = "Farm biomass by year") +
  prettyplot()

p3 <- ggplot(option_2_changes, aes(x = start_year, y = farm_food_prov, colour = country)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.75) +
  labs(x = "Start year", y = "Feed provided", title = "Feed provided by year") +
  prettyplot()

p4 <- ggplot(option_2_changes, aes(x = farm_biomass, y = farm_food_prov, colour = country)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.75) +
  labs(x = "Farm biomass", y = "Feed provided", title = "Feed provided by farm biomass") +
  prettyplot()

(p1 + p2) / (p3 + p4) +
  plot_layout(guides = "collect") &
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
Run the model with all species parameters and target harvested biomass fixed through time, with population allowed to change to adapt with the climate 
# ftp <- farm_temps_pops[[1]]
option_3_changes <- map_dfr(farm_temps_pops, function (ftp) {
  # start_year <- (2025:2098)[1]
  map_dfr(2025:2097, function(start_year) {
    ftpy <- ftp %>%
      filter(year %in% c(start_year, start_year + 1)) %>% 
      mutate(
        doy = as.integer(format(date, "%j")),
        doy = case_when(
          year == start_year ~ doy, 
          leap_year(date) ~ doy + 366,
          T ~ doy + 365
        )
      ) %>% 
      filter(doy >= doy_start)
    ftpy <- ftpy[1:species_params["gd"], ] # required because some years are leap years, so the end date will be a little different
    times <- c(t_start = unique(ftpy$doy_start), t_end = (unique(ftpy$doy_start) + nrow(ftpy) - 1), dt = 1L)
    
    results_full <- farm_growth_full(
      species_params = species_params, 
      feed_params = feed_params, 
      water_temp = ftpy$sst, 
      times = times,
      use_MC_population = F
    )
    results_consolidate <- consolidate_farm(results_full)

    # Adapt population to absorb the changes in individual size
    harvest_ind_weight <- last(results_consolidate[["stats"]][["weight"]][,1])
    target_biomass <- unique(ftpy$prod_perfarm) %>% set_units("t") %>% set_units("g") %>% drop_units()
    N_pop_adj <- generate_pop(
      harvest_n = target_biomass/harvest_ind_weight, 
      mort = species_params["mortmyt"], 
      times = times
    )

    results_farm <- scale_farm_population(
      consolidated = results_consolidate, 
      N_pop = N_pop_adj
    )

    data.frame(
      start_year = start_year,
      population = target_biomass/harvest_ind_weight,
      farm_biomass = last(results_farm[["stats"]][["weight_scaled"]][,1]),
      farm_food_prov = sum(results_farm[["stats"]][["food_prov_scaled"]][,1], na.rm = T)
    )
  }) %>% 
    mutate(
      country = unique(ftp$country),
      farm_ID = unique(ftp$farm_ID)
    )
},
.progress = T
)

qd_save(
  option_3_changes,
  file.path(data_path, "intermediates", "option_3_changes.qs")
)

As expected, population increases with increasing SST (Figure 4). Feed provided by year also increases with SST, although much less dramatically. In combination, feed provided per harvested biomass increases slightly with increasing SST, likely due to the decrease in mean fish weight and the (marginally) less efficient use of feed by smaller individuals.

Look at option 3 outcomes 
option_3_changes <- file.path(data_path, "intermediates", "option_3_changes.qs") %>% 
  qd_read() %>%  
  mutate(country = as.factor(country))

p1 <- ggplot(option_3_changes, aes(x = start_year, y = population, colour = country)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.75) +
  labs(x = "Start year", y = "Farm population", title = "Farm population by year") +
  prettyplot()

p2 <- ggplot(option_3_changes, aes(x = start_year, y = farm_biomass, colour = country)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.75) +
  labs(x = "Start year", y = "Farm biomass", title = "Farm biomass by year") +
  prettyplot()

p3 <- ggplot(option_3_changes, aes(x = start_year, y = farm_food_prov, colour = country)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.75) +
  labs(x = "Start year", y = "Feed provided", title = "Feed provided by year") +
  prettyplot()

p4 <- ggplot(option_3_changes, aes(y = farm_food_prov/farm_biomass, x = country, fill = country)) +
  geom_boxplot(colour = "black") +
  labs(x = "Farm biomass", y = "Feed provided/farmed biomass", title = "Feed provided by farm biomass") +
  prettyplot() +
  coord_flip()

(p1 + p2) / (p3 + p4) +
  plot_layout(guides = "collect") &
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
